coredata.dc:title: Adsorption of toxic carbamate pesticide oxamyl from liquid phase by newly synthesized and characterized graphene quantum dots nanomaterials
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Agarwal, Shilpi
Sadeghi, Nima
Tyagi, Inderjeet
Gupta, Vinod Kumar
Fakhri, Ali
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots have been synthesized using the microwave-assisted hydrothermal route. The surface textural and morphological structure of synthesized adsorbent i.e. graphene quantum dots was analyzed using various analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction, Transmission electron Microscopy, Atomic Force Microscopy and N2 adsorption-desorption instrumental techniques. The application of graphene quantum dots as an adsorbent for the removal of noxious pesticide compound i.e. oxamyl from aqueous solutions was well investigated and elucidated. The impact of several effective parameters such as effect of agitation speed, pH, adsorbent dose, contact time, temperature and initial concentration on sorption efficiency was studied and optimized using batch adsorption experiments. The optimized pH for maximum oxamyl adsorption was found to be 8.0 and for the maximum adsorption rates the adsorbent dose of 0.6g was found to be optimum to carry out the adsorption with in less than 25min of contact time. From the results obtained, it is clear that for all contact times, an increase in oxamyl concentration resulted in increase in the percent oxamyl removal. The adsorption equilibrium and kinetic data were well fitted and found to be in good agreement with the Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order kinetic model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Oxamyl
Isotherms
Kinetic models
Thermodynamic study

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mass transfer aspects of oxamyl adsorption onto GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge supporting of this research by the Young Researchers and Elites club, Islamic Azad University, Science and Research Branch .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern (A), AFM (B), TEM images (C), distribution particles histogram (D) and Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm curve (E and F) of GQDs.
UV–vis (A) and PL (B) spectra of GQDs.

Effects of (A) agitation speed (C=100mg/L; T=20°C; pH=8; adsorbent dose=0.6g), (B) contact time (Agitation speed=250rpm; pH=8; T=20°C; adsorbent dose=0.6g), (C) initial pH solution (Agitation speed=250rpm; C=50 and 150mg/L; T=20°C; adsorbent dose=0.6g), (D) adsorbent dosage (Agitation speed=250rpm; pH=8; T=20°C; C=100mg/L) on the adsorption of oxamyl.
Langmuir isotherm (Type 1), (Type 2) plots of oxamyl adsorption onto GQDs.
Pseudo-second-order plot of oxamyl adsorption onto GQDs.
Plot of Gibbs free energy change vs. temperature for adsorption of oxamylonto GQDs.
Isotherm parameters for the adsorption of oxamyl by GQDs.
Kinetic parameters for adsorption of oxamyl onto GQDs.
Mass transfer parameters for the adsorption of oxamyl by GQDs.
Thermodynamic parameters for adsorption of oxamyl onto GQDs.
A comparative study of various adsorption capacities for noxious oxamyl.

head.ce:title: Adsorption of toxic carbamate pesticide oxamyl from liquid phase by newly synthesized and characterized graphene quantum dots nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, University of Johannesburg, Johannesburg, South Africa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, 247667, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Young Researchers and Elites Club, Science and Research Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, South Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, 247667, India (V.K. Gupta). Young Researchers and Elites Club, Science and Research Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran (A. Fakhri).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Morphological and anatomical property of the synthesized graphene quantum dot nanomaterials and chemical structure of oxamyl impurity.

Graphene quantum dots have been synthesized using the microwave-assisted hydrothermal route. The surface textural and morphological structure of synthesized adsorbent i.e. graphene quantum dots was analyzed using various analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction, Transmission electron Microscopy, Atomic Force Microscopy and N2 adsorption-desorption instrumental techniques. The application of graphene quantum dots as an adsorbent for the removal of noxious pesticide compound i.e. oxamyl from aqueous solutions was well investigated and elucidated. The impact of several effective parameters such as effect of agitation speed, pH, adsorbent dose, contact time, temperature and initial concentration on sorption efficiency was studied and optimized using batch adsorption experiments. The optimized pH for maximum oxamyl adsorption was found to be 8.0 and for the maximum adsorption rates the adsorbent dose of 0.6g was found to be optimum to carry out the adsorption with in less than 25min of contact time. From the results obtained, it is clear that for all contact times, an increase in oxamyl concentration resulted in increase in the percent oxamyl removal. The adsorption equilibrium and kinetic data were well fitted and found to be in good agreement with the Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order kinetic model.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of large quantities of pesticides in agriculture practices is one of the main causes of soil pollution. Soil acts like an active filter, where chemical compounds are degraded by physical, chemical, and biological processes [1]. Both the accumulation of pesticides in the soil and their dispersion in the environment depend chiefly on the characteristics and overall functioning of the ecosystem. The concentration of these contaminants usually found in continental waters is quite low (in the range of μg−1 or less) and very often may be broken down, or degraded, by the action of sunlight, hydrolysis or micro-organisms [2,3].

Oxamyl (N,N-dimethylcarbamoyloxyimino-2-(methylthio)acetamide) is a main carbamoyl oxime insecticide nematicide which is extensively used in India for controlling the growth of nematodes in vegetable crops. It is a colorless crystalline solid with a melting point of 100–102°C changing to a dimorphic form with a melting point of 108–110°C. It has a slightly sulphur like odour and has a specific gravity of 0.97 (25°/4°). Due to its high water solubility product (Ksp =280gL−1) and low soil sorption coefficient value (Koc =25), it is defined as an extremely noxious compound having an acute oral lethal dose (LD50 =3.1mgkg−1 for male rat). It possesses severe detrimental and toxicological effects on the prevailing flora and fauna of the particular ecosystem as well as in living beings. If it enters the biological cycle of the human, it inhibits the acetylcholinesterase by the rapid carbamylation of its active site [4,5]. It is somewhat less toxic for the fishes living in that particular aquatic ecosystem and it induces a concentration dependent increase in genomic DNA damage. Several other side effects include Malaise, osteoporosis, excessive sweating, nausea, abdominal pain, and Miosis with unclear vision [6–8].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are a new member of the carbon nanomaterial family, have attracted considerable scientific attention due to their unique, excellent physical and chemical properties. Owing to better quantum confinement, more edge defects, high aqueous solubility, strong photoluminescence (PL) emission, desirable fluorescence properties and low cytotoxicity, GQDs have been developed for applications in various fields, such as photocatalysis [7], biosensing [8] and chemiluminescence analysis [9].

GQDs can be prepared using two strategies, including the top-down method and the bottom-up growth process. Most top-down methods include cutting graphene oxide (GO) by a hydrothermal method [10] and graphitic exfoliation by an electrochemical route [11].

In the present work, the adsorption characteristics of oxamyl were investigated using GQDs as an adsorbent. The adsorption isotherms, thermodynamics, and kinetics of the adsorbent were measured. Equilibrium, thermodynamic and kinetic parameters were calculated to determine the adsorption mechanism. The GQDs has several properties such as high surface area, high stability, and low cost. Hence this material plays an important role in adsorption study for removal of noxious oxamyl compound.

In this study, chemicals i.e. citric acid, tetrahydrofuran (THF), oxamyl, HCl and NaOH were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Ltd. Stock solution and all working solutions were prepared with deionized water. All reagents used in the study were of analytical grade. Prior use, all glassware was cleaned with dilute nitric acid and repeatedly washed with deionized water.

The citric acid was heated by convectional microwave oven a certain power (800W) for 20min to complete the pre-processing of precursor. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was dissolved by tetrahydrofuran (THF). After ultrasonic treatment for 2h, the mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 150°C for 6h. After the hydrothermal reaction, samples were washed dried in vacuum at 100°C for 1h.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by a Japan Rigaku D/max-3C using Cu Ka radiation. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained by tapping-mode on a Nanoscope IIIa (Digital Instruments) with NSC15 tips (silicon cantilever, Mikro Masch). The particle size was measured using Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (Zeiss EM-900). The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) was analyzed by nitrogen adsorption instrument in an ASAP2020 surface area. UV–vis and photoluminescence studies were performed using TEC Avaspec 2048 Spectrophotometer (excitation source=Xenon arc lamp 450W).

Batch mode adsorption studies for oxamyl have been carried out to investigate the effect of different parameters such as agitation speed, adsorbate concentration, adsorbent dose, temperature and pH. Solution containing 20ml adsorbate and 0.6g adsorbent was taken in 250ml capacity conical flask and agitated at 200rpm in water bath shaker at predetermined time intervals. The adsorbate solution was centrifuged at (3000rpm) for (5min). The initial and final Oxamyl concentrations remaining in solutions were analyzed by using the aid of a two dimensional Gas Chromatography (GC∗GC).

Adsorption efficiency was expressed as a percentage of adsorbed Oxamyl compared to initial Oxamyl concentration, whereas adsorption capacity was expressed as amount of Oxamyl adsorbed per mass unit of GQDs using the following equations, respectively:
(1)
q
( mg / g ) =
(
C
i
- C ) V
C
i
(2)
Oxamyl removal efficiency ( % ) =
C
i
- C
C
i
× 100
where Ci and C are the initial and residual concentrations of Oxamyl in mg/L, q is the adsorption capacity in mg/g, V is the volume of Oxamyl solution in L, and m is the adsorbent mass in g.

According to the XRD patterns presented in Fig.1 A, GQDs showed a wide diffraction peak at 2θ=27.8°, thereby suggesting the graphite structure of this sample. These results were confirmed by previous research [12].

The AFM result shows that the GQDs (Fig.1B) are mostly nanosheets of ∼10nm in size, whose height is mostly distributed in the range of 0.5–2.0nm (1.2nm average height). Although high quality TEM images are difficult to be obtained due to the low electro contrast between the GQDs and the carbon coated TEM grids, some nanosheets of ∼10nm in size can still be observed (Fig.1C). Based on the size distribution histogram shown in Fig.1D, the average diameter of the GQDs is about 10nm.

The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms for the GQDs were evaluated. The values of total pore volume, specific surface area and average pore size were 0.243cm3 g−1, 1221m2 g−1 and 4nm, respectively.

Raman spectroscopy was employed to characterize the structure of the GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2 . The G band corresponds to the first-order scattering of the E2g mode observed for sp2 carbon domains, and the D band is associated with the disorder band of a structural defect, amorphous carbon or an edge [13]. It is obviously see that two characteristic peaks, generally named D (∼1408cm−1) and G (∼1640cm−1) bands, were observed. The relative intensity of the “disorder” D-band to the crystalline G-band (ID/IG), as well as the sp3/sp2 carbon ratio for the GQDs were calculated to be approximately 0.68, which indicates that oxygen-containing groups were introduced to the edges and onto the basal plane of the GQDs [13].

The UV–vis absorption and PL spectrum of the prepared GQD is shown in Fig. 3 . As can be seen, an obvious absorbance edge of about 355nm and strong emission band at 455nm, which further confirms that the sp2 clusters contained in GQD even though these sp2 clusters were doped in the sp3 matrix should be uniform in size and the surface state. When the GQD were excited at 355nm, the GQD showed a strong fluorescence peak at 455nm. GQD exhibit PL peak in the visible region, which depends on the excitation wavelength.

The effect of agitation speed on adsorption of oxamyl was well studied in the range of 100–350rpm for GQDs adsorbent. The results are presented in Fig. 4 . From the results presented in Fig.4A, the maximum adsorption of oxamyl occurred at 250 for GQDs adsorbent. At low agitation speed, the adsorbent do not spread in the sample but gets accumulated. This will bury the active sites of the lower layer adsorbent and only the upper layer adsorbent active sites adsorb the oxamyl. This indicates that agitation rate should be sufficient to assure that all the surface binding sites are readily available for oxamyl uptake. But for further increase in agitation speed, amount removal decrease. This may be attributed to an increase in desorption tendency of adsorbate molecules.

The effect of the initial oxamyl concentration on the adsorption reaction by GQDs is well presented in Fig.4B. It can be seen that the amount of oxamyl adsorbed (mg/g) increased with increased initial concentration and remained constant after equilibrium time. The concentration provides an important driving force to overcome all mass transfer resistance of the oxamyl between the aqueous and solid phases. Hence a higher initial concentration of oxamyl will enhance the adsorption process. The equilibrium sorption capacity of the GQDs increased with an increase in the initial concentration. When the initial concentration increased from 50 to 150mg/L, the percentage removal increased from 42.50 to 95.70%. Fig.4B also indicated that the contact time needed for oxamyl solutions with initial concentrations of 50–150mg/L to reach equilibrium was less than 25min.

The effect of initial solution pH on oxamyl removal by GQDs is shown in Fig.4C. The adsorption of oxamyl increased with pH increasing from 2.0 to 8.0. The results indicated that at pH 8.0, the maximum adsorption capacity of 91.10 and 70.12mgg−1 occurred at an initial oxamyl concentration of 50 and 150mgL−1, respectively. The pH dependence of adsorption process can largely be related to the type and ionic state of functional groups present on the adsorbents and compound speciation in solution. At low pH (acidic) of the solution the carbon surface is predominantly positively charged, whereas at strongly basic pH, negative charges appear on the surface, due to the dissociation of the functional groups (carbonyl, hydroxyl, etc.). This results in a less positively or more negatively charged carbon surface at higher pH than at lower pH. Hence within the range of pH values from 8.0 to 12.0, the adsorption capacity decreased uniformly with increasing of pH. This indicates that the adsorption of the adsorbent is clearly pH dependent. The maximum adsorption capacity of the GQDs for oxamyl occurred at a pH value of around 8.

The effect of adsorbent dose was studied by varying the adsorbent quantity from 0.1 to 1.0g maintaining the initial oxamyl concentration; 50mgL−1, at constant temperatures; 20°C. The results are shown in Fig.4D. The %age adsorption increased with increasing adsorbent dose because of the increased availability of adsorption sites therefore in order to achieve maximum removal efficiency, 0.6g dose of adsorbent was sufficient for fixed initial concentration of oxamyl (50mgL−1). The adsorption amount (mg/g) increases with increase in adsorbent dosage, this might be due to the fact that some adsorbent sites are saturated at higher adsorbent dose.

In order to understand the effect of temperature on the influence of the removal of oxamyl by GQDs, experiments were carried out at temperatures of 20, 30 and 40°C, respectively, pH 8.0±0.3, and adsorbent dosage 0.6g. The removal efficiency increased with rise in temperature from 20 to 40°C at the oxamyl concentration 100mg/L (Figure not shown). The effect may be contributed to the weakening of hydrogen bonds and van der waals interaction at higher temperatures, resulting in the strengthening of physical interaction between the active sites of oxamyl and GQDs [14].

Adsorption isotherms are important to describe the adsorption mechanism of a solute on adsorbent surface thus aid in optimizing the design of a specific adsorption process. In the present study, the equilibrium data obtained for oxamyl removal using GQDs was tested with three isotherm models available in the literature to reveal the best fitting isotherm.

Langmuir isotherm [15–17] is represented in terms of fractional coverage (
θ
) as given by Eq. (3), and rearranged to the linear form given by Eq. (4) where KL is the adsorption equilibrium constant in L/mg related to the apparent energy of adsorption, qm is the quantity of adsorbate required to form a single monolayer on unit mass of adsorbent in mg/g, and qe is the amount adsorbed on unit mass of the adsorbent (mg/g) when the equilibrium concentration is Ce (mg/L).
(3)
θ =
q
e
q
m
=
K
L
C
e
( 1 +
K
L
C
e
)
(4)
C

q
e
=
1
K
L
q
m
+
C
e
q
m
(5)
1
q
e
=
1
q
m
+
1
K
L
q
m
C
e
The experimental data were plotted as Ce/qe vs. Ce (Type 1); 1/qe vs. 1/Ce (Type 2) and shown in Fig. 5 . The values of Langmuir constants qmax and KL were obtained by linear regression method and shown in Table 1 . The essential characteristic of the Langmuir isotherm can be expressed by the dimensionless constant called equilibrium parameter, RL, defined by [18]:
(6)
R
L
=
1
1 +
K
L
C
0
where C0 is the initial oxamyl concentration (mg/L). RL values indicate the type of isotherm to be irreversible (RL =0), favorable (0<RL <1), linear (RL =1) or unfavorable (RL >1).

The Freundlich isotherm is an empirical equation assuming that the adsorption process takes place on heterogeneous surfaces and adsorption capacity is related to the concentration of oxamyl at equilibrium. This isotherm model is defined by the equation [19] below:
(7)
q
e
=
K
F
C
e
1 / n
This equation is conveniently used in the following linear form:
(8)
ln
q
e
= ln
K
F
+
1
n
ln
C
e
where KF is Freundlich isotherm constant (L/g) and n is Freundlich isotherm exponent. Values of KF and n were calculated from the intercept and slope of plots ln qe vs. ln Ce and a straight line indicates the confirmation of the Freundlich isotherm for adsorption.

The generalized isotherm is a combination of Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms and depends on the value of cooperative binding constant (Nb). The linear form of the generalized isotherm is expressed in Eq. (9) where, KG is the saturation constant in mg/L, Nb is the cooperative binding constant, and qm is the maximum adsorption capacity of adsorbent in mg/g as obtained from the Langmuir isotherm model. Related isotherm constants are obtained from the slope and intercept of the plot of log [(qm/qe)−1] versus log Ce.
(9)
log
q
m
q
e
- 1
= log
K
G
=
N
b
log
C
e
However, since adsorption data are of a nonlinear nature, nonlinear regressions are also performed on each set of data points. The correlation coefficients obtained with both kinds of regression for four adsorption isotherms are summarized in Table 1. The correlation coefficient values in Table 1 indicate that the data fit the Langmuir isotherm better than the other isotherm.

The process of pollutant removal from an aqueous phase by any adsorbent can be explained by using kinetic models and examining the rate-controlling mechanism of the adsorption process. The study of adsorption kinetics is desirable as it provides information about the dynamics of the adsorption process in terms of the order and the rate constant which are of significance in designing and modelling an efficient adsorption operation. Various adsorption kinetic models have been used in the literature to describe the adsorption mechanism.

The Lagergren [20] rate equation is one of the most widely used adsorption rate equations for the adsorption of solute from a liquid solution. The pseudo-first-order kinetic model of Lagergren may be represented by
(10)
dq
t
dt
=
k
1
(
q
e
-
q
t
)
Integrating this equation for the boundary conditions t=0 to t=t and q=0 to q=qt, gives:
(11)
log (
q
e
-
q
t
) = log (
q
e
) -
k
1
2.303
t
where qe and qt are the amounts of oxamyl adsorbed (mg/g) at equilibrium and at time t (min), respectively, and k1 is the rate constant of pseudo-first-order adsorption (min−1). The validity of the model can be checked by linearized plot of log(qe −qt) versus t. The rate constant of pseudo-first-order adsorption is determined from the slope of the plot. The values of k1 and qe at different initial concentration are presented in Table 2 .

The pseudo-second-order equation based on adsorption equilibrium capacity can be expressed as [21]:
(12)
dq
t
dt
=
k
2
(
q
e
-
q
t
)
2
Taking into account, the boundary conditions t=0 to t=t and q=0 to q=qt, the integrated linear from of Eq. (12) can be represented to obtain Eq. (13):
(13)
t
q
t
=
1
k
2
q
e
2
+
t
q
e
A plot of t/qt vs. t (Fig. 6 ) gives a linear relationship, from which qe and k1 can be determined from the slope and intercept of the plot respectively.

Since neither the pseudo-first-order nor the second-order model can identify the diffusion mechanism, the kinetic results were analyzed by the intraparticle diffusion model to elucidate the diffusion mechanism, which model is expressed as [22]:
(14)
q =
k
i
t
1 / 2
+ C
where C is the intercept and ki is the intraparticle diffusion rate constant (mg/gmin1/2), which can be evaluated from the slope of the linear plot of qt versus t1/2.

Fractional power model is a modified form of the Freundlich model and the linearized form has been applied in previous studies [23]:
(15)
ln
q
t
= ln K + ν ln t
The product kv is defined as the specific adsorption rate a unit time.

Five kinetic models that were tested to explain the experimental data found. The agreement between experimental data and model calculated values is expressed by the correlation coefficient (R2). The results are presented in Table 2. Kinetic adsorption of oxamyl onto GQDs occurs with pseudo-second order model. In this fact, the higher values of R2 and the good agreement between the experimental and calculated equilibrium describes correctly the adsorption kinetics. So, the lower values of R2 and the difference of experimental and calculated equilibrium shows that the other model failed to describe the adsorption kinetics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mass transfer aspects of oxamyl adsorption onto GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Both external mass transfer model and intra-particle mass transfer diffusion model (Eqs. (16) and (17), respectively) are used for dynamic analysis of oxamyl adsorption onto GQDs.
(16)
d
C
C
0
dt
t → 0
= -
B
L
S
(17)
f
q
t
q
e
= -
log
1 -
q
t
q
e
2
=
4
π
2
D
i
t
2.3
d
2
where BL is the external mass transfer coefficient (cm/s) and S is the specific surface area for mass transfer. C and C0 is the concentration of solute in solution and initial concentration (mg/L), respectively. Di is diffusion coefficient in the solid (cm2/s) and d is the particle diameter. The values of BL and Di for different concentration were determined from the plots of C/C0 versus t and log [1−(qt/qe)2] versus t, respectively (Figure not shown) and the results are presented in Table 3 . As shown in Table 3, the BL values increased when concentration increased 50–150mg/L. The BL values suggest that the velocity of oxamyl transport from the bulk solution to the solid phase is quite rapid. The Di values are also increased when concentration increased 50–150mg/L. The increase in mass transfer coefficients with increasing concentration is due to enhanced diffusion of oxamyl in the boundary layer and a decrease in retarding forces acting on the diffusion ions as a consequence of higher thermal agitation when concentration increased 50–150mg/L.

The pseudo-second order model was identified as the best kinetic model for the adsorption of oxamyl on GQDs surface. Accordingly, the rate constants (k2) of the pseudo-second order model were adopted to calculate the activation energy of the adsorption process using the Arrhenius equation [24]:
(18)
ln
k
2
= ln A -
E
a
RT
where k2, A, Ea, R and T are the rate constant of the pseudo-second order model (gmg−1 min−1), the Arrhenius factor, the activation energy (kJ/mol), the gas constant (8.314J/molK) and the temperature (K), respectively. The activation energy could be determined from the slope of the plot of lnk2 versus 1/T (Figure not shown). The activation energy was 131.09, 117.01, 105.05kJ/mol at T=293K for oxamyl concentration 50, 100, 150mg/L, respectively. The magnitude of the activation energy yields information on whether the adsorption is mainly physical or chemical. The physic-sorption processes usually have energies in the range of 5–40kJmol−1, while higher activation energies (40–800kJmol−1) suggest chemisorption [25,26]. The value of activation energy given confirms the nature of the chemisorption process of oxamyl onto GQDs adsorbent (see Table 4 )

Thermodynamic considerations of an adsorption process are necessary to conclude whether the process is spontaneous or not. The experimental data obtained at different temperatures were used in calculating the thermodynamic parameters such as Gibbs free energy change (ΔG°), enthalpy change (ΔH°) and entropy change (ΔS°). The Gibbs free energy change of the sorption reaction is given by the following [25]:
(19)
Δ
G
0
= - RT ln
K
c
(20)
Δ
G
0
= Δ
H
0
- T Δ
S
0
where Kc is the equilibrium constant, which can be obtained from Langmuir isotherm, R is the universal gas constant, 8.314Jmol−1 K−1, and T is absolute temperature (K). ΔH° and ΔS° were obtained from the slope and intercept of the plot of Gibbs free energy change, ΔG° vs. temperature, T (Fig. 7 ). The calculated results are reported in Table 4.

The negative values of ΔG° confirm the feasibility of the process and the spontaneous nature of adsorption with a high preference for oxamyl onto GQDs. The value of ΔH° is positive, indicating that the adsorption reaction is endothermic. The positive value of ΔS ° reflects an increase in the randomness at the solid/solution interface during the adsorption process [25–37,39–42].

The adsorption capacity (Values of Qmax derived from Langmuir equation) of newly synthesized graphene quantum dots with other previously activated carbon [38] is presented in Table 5 . From the comparable data of the adsorption capacity it is clear that the newly synthesized graphene quantum dots possess superior adsorption capacity with respect to the previously developed adsorbents.

Novel graphene quantum dots were applied as adsorbent for the rapid removal and fast adsorption of the toxic oxamyl from the aqueous solution. From the results obtained it is clear that the adsorption of oxamyl is found to be more effective at the higher pH range i.e. 8,and for the maximum adsorption rates the adsorbent dose of 0.6g was found to be optimum to carry out the adsorption with in less than 25min of contact time. In addition, the very fast adsorption and settling for the GQDs make this material suitable for continuous flow water treatment systems. The adsorption equilibrium and kinetic data were well fitted and was found to be in good agreement with the Type I Langmuir isotherm model and pseudo-second order kinetic model with high value for correlation coefficient i.e. R2 =0.9999 and 0.9983 respectively. The adsorption of oxamyl dependence on temperature was investigated and the thermodynamic parameters ΔG°, ΔH° and ΔS ° were investigated and studied. The results obtained reveal the naturally feasible, spontaneous and endothermic adsorption process and on the other hand the mechanism of adsorption includes mainly ionic interactions (chemical interactions) and electrostatic interactions (physical interactions) between oxamyl cation and GQDs. According to Activation energy calculated, adsorption is a chemisorption process.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge supporting of this research by the Young Researchers and Elites club, Islamic Azad University, Science and Research Branch.
